back: A Brownian motion has independent increments, Gaussian increments B(s+t)-B(s) with mean 0 and variance t, and almost surely continuous sample paths. Exam use: Use as the base checklist whenever a process is claimed to be Brownian. Pitfall: Independent Gaussian increments alone do not automatically give continuous paths unless a continuous modification is specified.

The process {B_t-B_0:t>=0} is independent of B_0 and has the law of Brownian motion started at 0. Exam use: Lets hitting and exit calculations reduce to a Brownian motion started at 0 or at the current location. Pitfall: This is spatial translation of the starting point, not time-shift Markov conditioning.

For c>0 and B_0=0, {B_{cs}:s>=0} has the same finite-dimensional distributions as {sqrt(c) B_s:s>=0}. Exam use: High-yield tool for hitting-time laws, path regularity, and rescaling exit probabilities. Pitfall: Scaling changes time by c and space by sqrt(c); reversing these powers is a common error.

A continuous Gaussian process with mean 0 and covariance E[B_s B_t]=s wedge t is Brownian motion started at 0. Exam use: Use to prove transformed processes are Brownian, especially time inversion. Pitfall: The covariance condition alone is not enough without the Gaussian finite-dimensional law and continuity.

Brownian finite-dimensional distributions are built from Gaussian transition densities and then extended consistently to a path-space probability law. Exam use: Explains why the formal process exists before path properties are studied. Pitfall: The raw product path space can make continuity nonmeasurable; the continuous-path space fixes the measurable model.

If E|X_t-X_s|^beta <= K|t-s|^{1+alpha}, then dyadic paths admit a Holder-continuous version of any exponent gamma<alpha/beta. Exam use: Use moment bounds to prove sample-path continuity for Gaussian and process constructions. Pitfall: The exponent comes from alpha/beta, not beta/alpha; endpoint exponents are usually not obtained.

front: Chapter 7: Brownian Holder continuity below one half
back: Brownian paths are almost surely Holder continuous on compact intervals for every exponent gamma<1/2. Exam use: Useful for justifying pathwise approximations while remembering Brownian paths are rough. Pitfall: The exponent 1/2 itself is not included.

front: Chapter 7: Brownian paths are nowhere differentiable
back: With probability one, Brownian paths are not Lipschitz continuous, and hence not differentiable, at any time. Exam use: Prevents calculus-style manipulation of B_t and motivates quadratic variation and Ito calculus. Pitfall: Continuity does not imply bounded variation or differentiability; Brownian paths are continuous but very rough.

Along dyadic partitions of [0,t], the sum of squared Brownian increments converges to t in the sense used later for Ito's formula. Exam use: Core intuition behind the extra (1/2)f'' term in Ito's formula. Pitfall: This is about squared increments; ordinary total variation behaves very differently.

A d-dimensional Brownian motion has independent one-dimensional Brownian coordinates and Gaussian transition density (2 pi t)^(-d/2) exp(-|y-x|^2/(2t)). Exam use: Use for vector Ito formulas and multidimensional hitting or transition-density computations. Pitfall: Do not replace coordinate independence by merely matching each marginal coordinate law.

Conditioned on the present, the shifted future of Brownian motion has the law of Brownian motion started from the current state and is independent of the past. Exam use: Use to restart Brownian motion at deterministic times and compute conditional expectations. Pitfall: The conditioning sigma-field is the completed right-continuous past; using an informal past can hide null-set issues.

For bounded path functionals, conditioning on F_s^+ agrees almost surely with conditioning on the raw past F_s^0. Exam use: Clarifies why immediate-future events can be handled cleanly at time 0. Pitfall: This is an almost-sure equality of conditional expectations, not equality of all raw sigma-fields literally.

Every event in the Brownian germ sigma-field F_0^+ has probability 0 or 1 under a fixed starting point. Exam use: Use for immediate hitting, local oscillation, and path behavior as t downarrow 0. Pitfall: The probability may depend on the starting point; the law is not automatically uniform in x.

front: Chapter 7: Immediate crossing at the origin
back: Starting from 0, Brownian motion hits (0,infinity), hits (-infinity,0), and returns to 0 immediately with probability one. Exam use: Useful when proving the zero set has no isolated points and when reasoning about local extrema. Pitfall: The return time is inf{t>0:B_t=0}; allowing t=0 would make the statement trivial.

If B starts at 0, then X_0=0 and X_t=t B_{1/t} for t>0 is again Brownian motion. Exam use: Transfers small-time Brownian statements into large-time statements. Pitfall: The transform is t B_{1/t}, not B_{1/t}/t.

Events determined by the arbitrarily far future have probability identically 0 or identically 1 for all starting states. Exam use: Use for recurrence and unbounded oscillation at infinity. Pitfall: This is stronger than the germ law because the conclusion is uniform in the starting state.

One-dimensional Brownian motion satisfies limsup B_t/sqrt(t)=infinity and liminf B_t/sqrt(t)=-infinity almost surely. Exam use: Quick route to recurrence and repeated level-crossing arguments. Pitfall: This is weaker than the law of the iterated logarithm but enough for recurrence.

For every starting point, one-dimensional Brownian motion hits 0 infinitely often at arbitrarily large times almost surely. Exam use: Use whenever hitting times of points are assumed finite or when restarting at returns. Pitfall: Recurrence in one dimension does not generalize unchanged to higher-dimensional hitting of points.

A random time S is a stopping time when {S<t} is in F_t for every t; for right-continuous filtrations this is equivalent to using {S<=t}. Exam use: Use to verify that hitting, exit, minima, and approximating times are legitimate restart times. Pitfall: The definition is about information available by time t, not about whether S has a density or finite expectation.

front: Chapter 7: Open and closed hitting times are stopping times
back: For continuous Brownian paths, the first hitting time of an open set or a closed set is a stopping time. Exam use: Use to justify applying strong Markov property to level hitting and interval exit times. Pitfall: For general Borel sets the statement is true but much harder; do not use the open/closed proof blindly.

Increasing and decreasing limits of stopping times are stopping times in a right-continuous filtration. Exam use: Useful for proving complicated first-entry times by approximation. Pitfall: Right-continuity matters for some <= formulations.

front: Chapter 7: Random shift and information at a stopping time
back: The shift theta_S removes the path before S and restarts time at S; F_S contains events whose restriction to {S<=t} is F_t-measurable for every t. Exam use: Needed to state the strong Markov property and optional stopping cleanly. Pitfall: F_S is not simply F_t with t replaced by a random variable; it has its own measurability definition.

front: Chapter 7: Stopped value is known at the stopping time
back: If S is a stopping time, then B_S is F_S-measurable. Exam use: Lets conditional expectations at stopping times depend on the random current position B_S. Pitfall: This uses continuity and the completed/right-continuous filtration setup.

Given a stopping time S, the post-S Brownian path conditioned on F_S has the law of Brownian motion started at B_S, on {S<infinity}. Exam use: The central engine for reflection principle, hitting-time increments, recurrence refinements, and exit calculations. Pitfall: Always check S is a stopping time and restrict or control the event {S<infinity}.

The zero set of one-dimensional Brownian motion is almost surely closed, has no isolated points, is uncountable, and has Lebesgue measure zero. Exam use: Good conceptual anchor for path-property problems involving returns to zero. Pitfall: Uncountable does not imply positive Lebesgue measure.

front: Chapter 7: Level hitting times have stationary independent increments
back: Under P_0, the process {T_a:a>=0}, where T_a=inf{t:B_t=a}, has stationary independent increments as a function of the level a. Exam use: Use to recognize hitting times as a stable subordinator of index 1/2. Pitfall: The parameter is the spatial level a, not the Brownian time parameter.

For a>0, T_a has the same distribution as a^2 T_1. Exam use: Quickly reduces level-dependent hitting-time questions to the case a=1. Pitfall: The square on a is essential; hitting times scale like distance squared.

The Laplace transform phi_a(lambda)=E_0 exp(-lambda T_a) has the exponential form exp(-a c(lambda)), and eventually c(lambda)=sqrt(2 lambda). Exam use: Use before or after exponential martingales to compute exact transforms. Pitfall: The constant sqrt(2) requires the martingale calculation; scaling alone gives only proportionality.

For a>0, P_0(T_a<t)=2 P_0(B_t>=a), where T_a is the first hitting time of level a. Exam use: Essential for maxima distributions, hitting-time densities, and barrier-crossing probabilities. Pitfall: The result is for Brownian motion without drift and with a one-sided level; drift or two-sided barriers need changes.

For a>0, T_a has density a (2 pi s^3)^(-1/2) exp(-a^2/(2s)) on s>0. Exam use: Use for explicit integrals involving first passage times. Pitfall: This density has heavy tail and infinite mean; do not apply finite-mean intuition.

front: Chapter 7: Arcsine law for the last zero before one
back: If L=sup{t<=1:B_t=0}, then P_0(L<=s)=(2/pi) arcsin(sqrt(s)) for 0<s<1. Exam use: Classic Brownian path distribution and a bridge to random-walk arcsine laws. Pitfall: The density blows up near 0 and 1; the last zero is not concentrated near 1/2.

front: Chapter 7: Optional stopping for bounded stopping times
back: A right-continuous martingale stopped at a bounded stopping time has the same expectation as at time 0. Exam use: Foundation for Brownian exit probabilities and expected exit times. Pitfall: Boundedness or another valid stopping hypothesis is required; optional stopping is not automatic for unbounded times.

front: Chapter 7: Brownian motion is a martingale
back: B_t is a martingale with respect to the Brownian filtration. Exam use: Use with optional stopping to compute two-sided hitting probabilities. Pitfall: Requires integrability and the correct filtration.

For a<x<b, P_x(T_a<T_b)=(b-x)/(b-a). Exam use: High-yield gambler's-ruin analogue for Brownian motion. Pitfall: First show or know the exit time is finite; then justify the stopping/localization step.
B_t^2-t is a martingale. Exam use: Use to compute expected exit times and quadratic variation intuition. Pitfall: B_t^2 alone is a submartingale, not a martingale.

front: Chapter 7: Expected exit time from an interval
back: If T=inf{t:B_t notin (a,b)} with a<0<b and B_0=0, then E_0 T=-ab. Exam use: Standard computation for Brownian mean exit time from a bounded interval. Pitfall: Do not confuse exit from (a,b) with hitting a single level; the one-sided hitting time has infinite mean.

For real theta, exp(theta B_t - theta^2 t/2) is a martingale. Exam use: Use for Laplace transforms of hitting times and drift/barrier calculations. Pitfall: The compensator is theta^2 t/2; missing the factor 1/2 breaks the martingale.

front: Chapter 7: Exact Laplace transform of a hitting time
back: For T_a=inf{t:B_t=a} and a>0, E_0 exp(-lambda T_a)=exp(-a sqrt(2 lambda)). Exam use: Fastest exact calculation for first-passage Laplace transforms. Pitfall: The formula is for one-sided hitting from 0 to a>0; signs and starting points need translation.

If u_t+(1/2)u_xx=0 for a suitable polynomial u(t,x), then u(t,B_t) is a martingale. Exam use: Generates polynomial martingales such as B_t^3-3tB_t and B_t^4-6tB_t^2+3t^2. Pitfall: The sign is the backward heat equation sign; check whether the time variable is forward or backward in a problem.

front: Chapter 7: Second moment of symmetric exit time
back: For T=inf{t:B_t notin (-a,a)}, E[T^2]=5a^4/3. Exam use: Useful template for computing higher exit-time moments via polynomial martingales. Pitfall: In nonsymmetric intervals, T and B_T^2 need not be independent, so the same shortcut fails.

For f in C^2, f(B_t)-f(B_0)=int_0^t f'(B_s)dB_s + (1/2)int_0^t f''(B_s)ds. Exam use: The central calculus rule for functions of Brownian motion. Pitfall: Ordinary chain rule misses the second-derivative correction.

If g is continuous and E int_0^t |g(B_s)|^2 ds is finite, then int_0^t g(B_s)dB_s is a continuous martingale. Exam use: Lets Ito formula immediately produce martingales when the drift term vanishes. Pitfall: Square-integrability is the hypothesis that keeps the stochastic integral controlled.

For f in C^2 in time and space, df(t,B_t)=f_t(t,B_t)dt+f_x(t,B_t)dB_t+(1/2)f_xx(t,B_t)dt. Exam use: Use to connect Brownian martingales with PDEs and exponential models. Pitfall: There is no term involving dt dB_t or (dt)^2 in the limit.

front: Chapter 7: Ito formula for exponential Brownian motion
back: For X_t=exp(mu t+sigma B_t), Ito's formula gives dX_t=(mu+sigma^2/2)X_t dt+sigma X_t dB_t. Exam use: Common model pattern for geometric Brownian motion and for checking exponential martingales. Pitfall: The drift of exp(mu t+sigma B_t) is mu+sigma^2/2, not just mu.

For d-dimensional Brownian motion, df(t,B_t)=f_t dt+sum_i f_i dB_t^i+(1/2)sum_i f_{ii} dt. Exam use: Use for radial processes, harmonic functions, and multidimensional martingales. Pitfall: The Laplacian term appears with factor 1/2; mixed second derivatives do not contribute for standard independent Brownian coordinates.
